settings.title: We Now Install Native Wildflower Meadows
settings.excerpt: Our new meadow service swaps thirsty lawn for a low-water mix that feeds local bees from May to October.
settings.body:
Lawns are lovely until July, when they need mowing every week and watering every other day. This year we added a service we have wanted to offer for a long time: native wildflower meadows.

We strip the turf, sow a regional seed mix of yarrow, oxeye daisy, and red clover, and walk you through the first season so you know what to expect. The first year looks sparse. The second year it sings. That gap catches people out, so we are upfront about it before any turf comes up.

How the install works

We start in autumn or early spring, when the soil is workable and the seed has time to settle before the warmth comes. Most lawn topsoil is too rich for wildflowers, so we often reduce its fertility first, which sounds odd until you see how the grasses stop bullying the flowers out.

  • We lift the existing turf and prepare a fine, firm seedbed
  • We sow a mix matched to your local soil and aspect
  • We agree a simple cutting plan for the first two years

A meadow needs cutting once or twice a year instead of weekly. The main cut comes in late summer, after the flowers have set seed, and we recommend leaving the cuttings a day or two so the seed drops before you clear them. It also brings in bees, hoverflies, and butterflies that a clipped lawn never will.

One honest tip: a meadow is not a no-effort lawn, it is a different kind of effort spread thinner across the year. If you want green carpet all summer, this is not it. If you want movement, colour, and a hum of insects, it is hard to beat.

Ask us for a meadow quote when we visit, and we will tell you honestly if your site suits it.
